column1: #separator:tab
#html:true
Front
column2: Back

column1: The Mel scale is a linear scale that maps frequency to pitch in speech recognition.
column2:
FALSE
Mel is a logarithmic/perceptual scale: Mel = 1127·ln(1 + f/700). It approximates human pitch perception.

Source: exam2024:Q1a, L04:mel

column1: The RNN-Transducer model is designed for offline decoding and does not support streaming speech recognition.
column2:
FALSE
RNN-T supports streaming/online decoding via the prediction network and joint network with blank tokens.

Source: exam2024:Q1b, L04:architectures

column1: According to the James-Lange theory, the perception of an emotion-arousing stimulus leads simultaneously to the experience of emotion and physiological (bodily) changes.
column2:
FALSE
James-Lange: Stimulus → Physiological response → Emotion (sequential). Simultaneous emotion + body is Cannon-Bard.

Source: exam2024:Q1c, solution_ex1:2a

column1: Using convex optimization, a skin conductance signal can be decomposed into phasic and tonic components.
column2:
TRUE
SC decomposition into fast phasic (event-related) and slow tonic (baseline) components uses convex optimization (lecture L02).

Source: exam2024:Q1d, L02:skin_conductance

column1: In the transformer model, positional encoding is used to provide the model with information about the order of tokens in the input sequence.
column2:
TRUE
Self-attention is permutation-invariant; positional encodings inject token position information.

Source: exam2024:Q1e, solution_ex1:3c

column1: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) can be used for tasks where the input is a sequence of tokens, such as language modeling and sequence classification.
column2:
TRUE
RNNs maintain hidden state across time steps — used for LM, sentiment classification, etc.

Source: exam2024:Q1f, L03:rnn

column1: Pruning involves removing the most impactful connections from a neural network.
column2:
FALSE
Pruning removes least important weights/neurons to compress the model while preserving performance.

Source: exam2024:Q1g, solution_ex2:2b

column1: Quantization reduces both the model size and the model accuracy.
column2:
TRUE
Quantization maps weights/activations to lower precision → smaller model, but typically some accuracy loss (though techniques like SmoothQuant mitigate this).

Source: exam2024:Q1h, L05:quantization

column1: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning operates only on a true subset of the model.
column2:
TRUE
PEFT (LoRA, adapters) freezes most weights and trains only a small subset of additional parameters.

Source: exam2024:Q1i, solution_ex2:1c

column1: The Griffin-Lim algorithm reconstructs the phase information iteratively from just the magnitude spectrogram without using any learned parameters.
column2:
TRUE
Griffin-Lim is an iterative phase-estimation algorithm — no neural network, uses magnitude spectrogram only.

Source: exam2024:Q1j, solution_ex3:3a

column1: A nuclear accent is the last accent in a phrase and is perceived as more prominent.
column2:
TRUE
The nuclear (phrase-final) accent is the most prominent accent in an intonation phrase.

Source: exam2024:Q1k, L08:prosody

column1: In FastSpeech, the speech speeds up when we repeat the encoder states by doubling the predicted duration.
column2:
FALSE
Repeating encoder states lengthens the output → speech slows down. Fewer repeats → faster speech.

Source: exam2024:Q1l, solution_ex3:2c

column1: Autonomous agents are characterized by their ability to operate independently, react to their environment, and plan proactively. They also have control over their internal states.
column2:
TRUE
Classic agent properties: autonomy, reactivity, pro-activeness, social ability (lecture L12).

Source: exam2024:Q1m, L12:agents

column1: Dialogue trees offer high flexibility in responding to unexpected queries, but they are difficult to scale and require manual design for new topics.
column2:
FALSE
Dialogue trees have low flexibility for unexpected queries; they are hard to scale (second part is true).

Source: exam2024:Q1n, solution_ex5:1b

column1: In a reinforcement learning algorithm, the reward should be differentiable so that we can run back-propagation to train the policy based on data.
column2:
FALSE
Rewards are not required to be differentiable. Policy gradients estimate gradients via sampled trajectories.

Source: exam2024:Q1o, solution_ex5:1e

column1: We must collect reinforcement learning trajectories from agent-environment interactions when employing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to learn the policy. The collected reinforcement learning trajectories can have various lengths.
column2:
TRUE
PPO is on-policy: collects rollouts from current policy; trajectories can vary in length.

Source: exam2024:Q1p, L14:ppo

column1: The animation principle "Slow In and Slow Out" emphasizes linear motion.
column2:
FALSE
Slow In / Slow Out means ease in and ease out — acceleration/deceleration at start and end, not constant linear speed.

Source: exam2024:Q1q, L10:principles

column1: Facial muscles attach to a bone and connect to the skin. When they contract, they create dynamic wrinkles that form perpendicular to the direction of muscle contraction.
column2:
TRUE
Facial muscles attach bone-to-skin; wrinkles form perpendicular to contraction direction (lecture L10).

Source: exam2024:Q1r, L10:anatomy

column1: Linear blend skinning can handle large joint rotation angles without visual artifacts.
column2:
FALSE
LBS causes candy-wrapper twisting and volume loss at large joint angles due to linear rotation blending.

Source: exam2024:Q1s, solution_ex4:2a5

column1: Artifact detection is essential in affective computing because physiological signals like EEG and ECG can be contaminated by noise from muscle movements or environmental interference.
column2:
TRUE
Biosignals need artifact removal (movement, EMG contamination, etc.) before reliable affect inference.

Source: exam2025:Q1a, L02:artifact_detection

column1: In backpropagation, the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight is computed independently of the activation functions used in the network.
column2:
FALSE
Backprop uses the chain rule — gradients depend on activation function derivatives (ReLU, sigmoid, etc.).

Source: exam2025:Q1b, L03:neural_nets

column1: Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) is commonly applied to short-time frames of a speech signal to analyze how frequency content changes over time.
column2:
TRUE
STFT: window speech → DFT per frame → spectrogram over time.

Source: exam2025:Q1c, L04:feature_extraction

column1: Prosody refers exclusively to the spectral features of speech, such as formants and harmonics, without involving rhythm or intonation.
column2:
FALSE
Prosody includes pitch, duration, loudness, rhythm, intonation — not just spectral features.

Source: exam2025:Q1d, solution_ex3:1c

column1: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning methods (PEFT) like LoRA and adapters reduce computational cost by freezing the majority of a pre-trained model's weights and learning only a small number of additional parameters.
column2:
TRUE
PEFT keeps most LLM weights frozen; trains low-rank adapters or small added layers.

Source: exam2025:Q1e, solution_ex2:1c

column1: Prompt engineering can be used to implicitly control the chatbot's tone, style, and persona without modifying the underlying model.
column2:
TRUE
System prompts, role prompting, and style instructions shape output without weight updates.

Source: exam2025:Q1f, L06:prompting

column1: Hybrid fusion strategies eliminate the need for modality-specific embedding by treating all modalities identically throughout the model pipeline.
column2:
FALSE
Hybrid fusion combines early and late fusion — still uses modality-specific encoders/embeddings.

Source: exam2025:Q1g, L07:multimodality

column1: Stress depends on sentence context, while accent is fixed within the word.
column2:
TRUE
Word accent is lexical (fixed); sentence stress/placement varies with context and emphasis.

Source: exam2025:Q1h, L08:prosody

column1: WaveNet uses causal, dilated convolutions to model long-range temporal dependencies in sequential data such as speech.
column2:
TRUE
WaveNet: stacked dilated causal convolutions for autoregressive waveform generation.

Source: exam2025:Q1i, solution_ex3:3a

column1: In Linear Blend Skinning, each vertex is influenced by exactly one bone.
column2:
FALSE
LBS blends influences from multiple bones with weights w_ij summing to 1.

Source: exam2025:Q1j, solution_ex4:1c

column1: Linear interpolation between animation key frames often results in unnatural or abrupt motion rather than smooth transitions.
column2:
TRUE
Pure linear interpolation lacks ease-in/out → mechanical motion; Disney's Slow In/Out fixes this.

Source: exam2025:Q1k, L10:principles

column1: In normalizing flows, the model learns only the prior distribution pZ, while the transformation functions remain fixed.
column2:
FALSE
Normalizing flows learn invertible transformation functions; change of variables adjusts density via Jacobian determinant.

Source: exam2025:Q1l, solution_ex4:1h

column1: An autonomous agent's actions are determined solely by its immediate perception of the environment.
column2:
FALSE
Agents also use internal state, goals, and proactive planning — not perception alone.

Source: exam2025:Q1m, solution_ex5:1a

column1: A primary disadvantage of dialogue trees is their limited flexibility, which makes it difficult for them to handle unexpected user queries or engage in open-ended conversations.
column2:
TRUE
Dialogue trees are rigid, manually authored, poor at open-domain unexpected input.

Source: exam2025:Q1n, solution_ex5:1b

column1: In on-policy reinforcement learning, data collected from older versions of the policy can be stored in a replay buffer and reused to improve training efficiency.
column2:
FALSE
On-policy methods (PPO) use current policy data only. Replay buffers are off-policy (DQN, SAC).

Source: exam2025:Q1o, L14:rl_algorithms

column1: In the Q-learning algorithm, the policy is implicitly derived by selecting the action with the highest estimated Q-value for a given state, rather than being represented by a separate, explicit policy network.
column2:
TRUE
Q-learning: greedy policy π(s) = argmax_a Q(s,a); no separate policy network needed.

Source: exam2025:Q1p, L14:value_based

column1: Facial expression analysis and blood pressure measurement are both invasive sensing methods.
column2:
FALSE
Facial analysis is non-invasive (video). Blood pressure measurement is invasive/interactive (cuff).

Source: solution_ex1:1a

column1: Action units refer to the motion of the whole body.
column2:
FALSE
AUs are facial muscle movements (FACS), not whole-body motion.

Source: solution_ex1:1b

column1: ECG refers to recording electrical activity captured from the skin's surface.
column2:
TRUE
ECG electrodes on skin record heart's electrical activity.

Source: solution_ex1:1c

column1: Softmax outputs are limited to the range [−1, 1].
column2:
FALSE
Softmax outputs are in [0, 1] and sum to 1 (probabilities).

Source: solution_ex1:1d

column1: RNNs process all time steps simultaneously like Transformers.
column2:
FALSE
RNNs process sequentially; Transformers use parallel self-attention.

Source: solution_ex1:1e

column1: The attention mechanism assigns different weights to different parts of the input.
column2:
TRUE
Attention computes a weighted sum — higher weight = more focus.

Source: solution_ex1:1f

column1: In ADC, quantization converts real-valued numbers to positive numbers only.
column2:
FALSE
Quantization maps to a finite discrete set — can include negative values.

Source: solution_ex1:1g

column1: The fundamental frequency is the highest frequency component of a complex waveform.
column2:
FALSE
Fundamental frequency is the lowest (most dominant periodic) frequency.

Source: solution_ex1:1h

column1: A Mel filter bank consists of overlapping rectangular filters.
column2:
FALSE
Mel filters are overlapping triangular filters spaced on the Mel scale.

Source: solution_ex1:1i

column1: Top-p sampling randomly picks from the p most likely words.
column2:
FALSE
Top-p (nucleus): smallest set whose cumulative prob ≥ p. Top-k picks k most likely.

Source: solution_ex2:1a

column1: More detailed prompts always lead to better AI-generated responses.
column2:
FALSE
Overly detailed/restrictive prompts can reduce creativity and flexibility.

Source: solution_ex2:1b

column1: PEFT reduces the total number of model parameters.
column2:
FALSE
PEFT trains a subset of params; total param count may increase (adapters) but compute cost drops.

Source: solution_ex2:1c

column1: In-context learning involves training with prompt-response pairs.
column2:
FALSE
ICL uses examples at inference without weight updates.

Source: solution_ex2:1d

column1: RAG supplements LLM responses with retrieved external information.
column2:
TRUE
RAG retrieves domain-specific/up-to-date docs to augment generation.

Source: solution_ex2:1e

column1: MOS and A/B testing are identical qualitative evaluation methods.
column2:
FALSE
MOS: absolute rating scale. A/B: pairwise preference comparison.

Source: solution_ex2:1f

column1: Multimodal chatbots can use user state modeling for more empathetic responses.
column2:
TRUE
Voice tone, facial expression, sentiment → adaptive empathetic responses.

Source: solution_ex2:1g

column1: Late fusion is always superior to early fusion.
column2:
FALSE
Choice depends on task; early fusion can capture cross-modal interactions better.

Source: solution_ex2:1h

column1: Chatbot personality uses the same five factors as the human Big Five model.
column2:
FALSE
Chatbot traits: artificiality, conscientiousness, vibrancy, civility, neuroticism.

Source: solution_ex2:1i

column1: Text normalization is only needed to expand abbreviations like Dr. and Mr.
column2:
FALSE
Also: number expansion, punctuation disambiguation, etc.

Source: solution_ex3:1a

column1: ML models can be trained to detect sentence boundaries from punctuation context.
column2:
TRUE
Period can be abbreviation vs. sentence end — ML can disambiguate.

Source: solution_ex3:1b

column1: Prosodic analysis includes loudness but not pitch.
column2:
FALSE
Prosody includes pitch (F0), duration, and loudness.

Source: solution_ex3:1c

column1: Prominence in speech refers to the volume of words only.
column2:
FALSE
Prominence involves pitch, duration, and sometimes loudness — emphasis.

Source: solution_ex3:1d

column1: Putting two prominent accents right next to each other is known as a lapse.
column2:
FALSE
Clash = accents too close. Lapse = accents too far apart.

Source: solution_ex3:1e

column1: Diphone synthesis often lacks natural prosody due to limited prerecorded units.
column2:
TRUE
Small diphone inventory → less natural prosody than unit selection.

Source: solution_ex3:1f

column1: Unit selection synthesis produces more natural speech by choosing larger contextually appropriate segments.
column2:
TRUE
Selects variable-length units from recorded speech for naturalness.

Source: solution_ex3:1g

column1: Concatenative synthesis generates speech by training neural networks on text.
column2:
FALSE
Concatenative/unit selection uses pre-recorded speech segments.

Source: solution_ex3:1h

column1: When SYS adds a word not in REF, that is a deletion error.
column2:
FALSE
Extra word in SYS = insertion. Missing word = deletion.

Source: solution_ex3:1i

column1: MOS uses an absolute scale while A/B tests ask for comparative preference.
column2:
TRUE
MOS: 1–5 quality rating. A/B: prefer A or B.

Source: solution_ex3:1j

column1: Stylized characters are more likely to trigger the uncanny valley than realistic characters.
column2:
FALSE
Realistic characters near-human are more uncanny; stylized avoids the valley.

Source: solution_ex4:1a

column1: In an articulated model, joints are only visual connectors between bones.
column2:
FALSE
Joints define degrees of freedom and how bones rotate/move.

Source: solution_ex4:1b

column1: In LBS, each vertex is influenced by exactly one bone.
column2:
FALSE
Vertices blend multiple bones with weights.

Source: solution_ex4:1c

column1: The Jacobian method is widely used for IK when analytical solutions aren't possible.
column2:
TRUE
Numerical IK via Jacobian pseudoinverse is standard.

Source: solution_ex4:1d

column1: Damping forces depend on spring stiffness.
column2:
FALSE
Damping depends on velocity; stiffness affects spring force.

Source: solution_ex4:1e

column1: Diverse movements are particularly important for stylized characters.
column2:
TRUE
Stylized animation emphasizes expressive, varied motion.

Source: solution_ex4:1f

column1: When m < d, manifold learning performs dimensionality reduction.
column2:
TRUE
High-dimensional data (d) projected to lower intrinsic dimension (m).

Source: solution_ex4:1g

column1: Normalizing flows use invertible functions; the Jacobian determinant adjusts density.
column2:
TRUE
Change of variables: p_X(x) = p_Z(f⁻¹(x)) · |det J_{f⁻¹}(x)|.

Source: solution_ex4:1h

column1: Proactive agents primarily respond to immediate environmental changes.
column2:
FALSE
Reactivity = immediate response. Proactiveness = planning for future opportunities.

Source: solution_ex5:1a

column1: Dialogue trees are highly scalable for new topics.
column2:
FALSE
New topics require manual design — poor scalability.

Source: solution_ex5:1b

column1: Link prediction determines whether a relationship should exist between two entities.
column2:
TRUE
Link prediction infers missing edges in a knowledge graph.

Source: solution_ex5:1c

column1: Knowledge graph embeddings can answer queries on large incomplete graphs.
column2:
TRUE
KGE enables similarity search and link prediction when SPARQL is insufficient.

Source: solution_ex5:1d

column1: In RL, the transition model must be differentiable.
column2:
FALSE
Transition model need not be differentiable; RL handles non-differentiable simulators.

Source: solution_ex5:1e

column1: Monte Carlo policy gradient requires on-policy trajectories.
column2:
TRUE
Policy gradient methods collect trajectories from the current policy.

Source: solution_ex5:1f

column1: The optimal policy maximizes each individual future reward step.
column2:
FALSE
Policy maximizes expected discounted return, not each individual reward.

Source: solution_ex5:1g

column1: Actor-Critic methods use a value function as baseline to reduce variance.
column2:
TRUE
Critic estimates V(s) or Q(s,a) to reduce variance of policy gradient estimates.

Source: solution_ex5:1h

column1: What is the sequence in the James-Lange theory of emotion?
column2:
Stimulus → Physiological response → Emotion
Example: see snake → heart races → feel fear.

Source: solution_ex1:2a, exam2024:Q1c

column1: What is the Cannon-Bard theory of emotion?
column2:
Physiological arousal and emotional experience occur simultaneously — both triggered by the stimulus in parallel (not sequentially).

Source: solution_ex1:2a, exam2025:Q2a

column1: What is the Schachter-Singer (two-factor) theory of emotion?
column2:
Emotion = physiological arousal + cognitive interpretation of the situation. Same arousal can yield different emotions depending on context.

Source: solution_ex1:2a, exam2025:Q2a

column1: In the dimensional model, define valence, arousal, and dominance.
column2:
Valence: pleasantness (+/−)
Arousal: intensity/activation
Dominance: sense of control vs. helplessness

Source: solution_ex1:2b

column1: Anxious, tense, and helpless — dimensional description?
column2:
Negative valence, high arousal, low dominance (feeling overwhelmed, not in control).

Source: exam2025:Q2b

column1: What are Action Units (AUs)?
column2:
Specific facial muscle movements from the Facial Action Coding System (FACS). Used to detect facial expressions linked to emotions.

Source: solution_ex1:1b, exam2024:Q2a

column1: What does HRV (heart rate variability) measure and what does higher HRV suggest?
column2:
HRV = variation in beat-to-beat intervals.
Higher HRV → more relaxed/adaptive autonomic state, often linked to positive emotion and stress resilience.

Source: solution_ex1:2d, exam2024:Q2b

column1: What does skin conductance (SC) measure?
column2:
Electrodermal activity — sweat gland activity reflecting sympathetic arousal. Used as arousal indicator in affective computing.

Source: exam2024:Q2b, L02:skin_conductance

column1: Name the four features of a phasic skin conductance response.
column2:
Amplitude — peak height
Latency — time from stimulus to response onset
Rise time — time to peak
Half-recovery time — time from peak to 50% recovery

Source: exam2024:Q2c, L02:skin_conductance

column1: How is SC decomposed into phasic and tonic components?
column2:
Using convex optimization to separate fast event-related phasic responses from slow tonic baseline drift.

Source: exam2024:Q1d, L02:skin_conductance

column1: Ekman's six basic emotions?
column2:
Joy, Sadness, Disgust, Surprise, Anger, Fear

Source: L02:basic_emotions

column1: Which Action Units indicate Happiness (Ekman/FACS)?
column2:
AU6 (Cheek Raiser) + AU12 (Lip Corner Puller)

Source: L02:AU_table

column1: Which Action Units indicate Surprise?
column2:
AU1 (Inner Brow Raiser) + AU2 (Outer Brow Raiser) + AU5 (Upper Lid Raiser) + AU26 (Jaw Drop)

Source: L02:AU_table

column1: Mouth Aspect Ratio (MAR) formula?
column2:
MAR = (|p₂−p₈| + |p₃−p₇| + |p₄−p₆|) / (3 · |p₅−p₁|)
Uses facial landmarks; measures mouth openness.

Source: L02:MAR, exam2024:Q2a

column1: How is fidgeting detected from video? (4 steps)
column2:
1. f_temp = f_gray − b_gray
2. Binarize f_temp (threshold)
3. Energy E = % surviving pixels
4. Update background: b = (1−α)b + α·f_gray

Source: L02:fidgeting, solution_ex1:2c

column1: Exercise 1 fidgeting: 9 pixels, 8 survive threshold. Compute Energy E (%).
column2:
E = (surviving pixels / total pixels) × 100 = 8/9 × 100 = 88.89%

Source: solution_ex1:2c

column1: What does OpenFace output?
column2:
68 facial landmarks, AU intensities [0,5], AU presence (binary), eye gaze, head pose.

Source: L02:OpenFace

column1: How are eye blinks detected?
column2:
Based on AU45 (eye blink), evaluated per frame — intensity and blink events.

Source: L02:eye_blinks

column1: Give examples of non-invasive vs. invasive emotion sensing.
column2:
Non-invasive: facial video, speech, text, ECG/SC on skin surface.
Invasive: hormone levels, neurotransmitter levels (blood/internal).

Source: L02:emotion_recognition, solution_ex1:1a

column1: ECG vs. PPG — what does each measure?
column2:
ECG: electrical activity of heart from skin electrodes.
PPG: light reflected by blood vessels (can use webcam on skin/pupil).

Source: L02:cardiac

column1: Name three time-domain HRV measures.
column2:
1. Standard deviation of R-R intervals
2. RMSSD (root mean square of successive differences)
3. pNN50 (% successive RR diffs > 50 ms)

Source: L02:HRV

column1: Exercise 1 HRV: seq1={72,75,70,73,76}. Compute mean and σ (std dev).
column2:
Mean x̄ = 366/5 = 73.2
σ = √(22.8/5) ≈ 2.14 → lower HRV, possibly stress/anxiety.

Source: solution_ex1:2d

column1: Exercise 1 HRV: seq2={74,71,76,79,80}. Compute σ.
column2:
Mean x̄ = 76
σ = √(54/5) ≈ 3.29 → higher HRV, relaxed/adaptive state.

Source: solution_ex1:2d

column1: Tonic vs. phasic skin conductance?
column2:
Tonic: slow baseline level (overall arousal).
Phasic: fast event-related responses (peaks after stimuli). Decomposed via convex optimization (cvxEDA).

Source: L02:skin_conductance, exam2024:Q1d

column1: Artifact detection for R-R intervals: define QD, MAD, MED, CBD.
column2:
QD = (Q3−Q1)/2
MAD = (Median Beat − 2.9·QD)/3
MED = 3.32·QD
CBD = (MAD + MED)/2
If |RRᵢ − last valid| > CBD → artifact.

Source: L02:artifact_detection, exam2025:Q2c

column1: Exam 2025: R-R interval 400 ms. Heart rate in bpm?
column2:
HR = 60000 / RR_ms = 60000/400 = 150 bpm (unusually high → suspicious).

Source: exam2025:Q2c-i

column1:
Exam 2025 artifact: RR={795,800,400,810,800,805}. Q1=795, Q3=805, median=800.
Compute CBD. Is 400 ms an artifact vs. last valid 800 ms?
Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) — what three dimensions?
column2:
Valence, Arousal, Dominance — used as ground truth for affect labeling.

Source: L02:SAM

column1: Boredom in the dimensional model?
column2:
Low valence (mildly unpleasant), low arousal (passive), medium-to-low dominance.

Source: solution_ex1:2b

column1: Video-based emotion recognition pipeline (4 stages)?
column2:
Preprocessing (frame rate, brightness) → Feature extraction (AUs, gaze, MAR, fidgeting) → Classification (valence/arousal) → Output

Source: L02:video_pipeline

column1: Basic emotions theory (Ekman) — three key claims?
column2:
1. Caused by phylogenetic event categories
2. Elicit physiological + expressive signals
3. Trigger innate action plans (fight/flight)

Source: L02:basic_emotions_theory

column1: What is core affect in dimensional emotion theory?
column2:
Most elementary affective feelings (valence + arousal) — part of but not identical to a full emotional episode (which also includes cognitive appraisal, action tendencies).

Source: L02:dimensional

column1: Biosensor pipeline for affect classification?
column2:
Raw signal → artifact detection → feature extraction (HRV stats, SC phasic/tonic) → classifier (e.g. Random Forest) → valence/arousal prediction.

Source: L02:biosensor_pipeline

column1: Fear Action Units (from lecture table)?
column2:
AU1 + AU2 + AU4 + AU5 + AU7 + AU20 + AU26 (brow raisers, lid raiser, lip stretcher, jaw drop, etc.)

Source: L02:AU_table

column1: Anger Action Units?
column2:
AU4 + AU5 + AU7 + AU23 (Brow Lowerer, Upper Lid Raiser, Lid Tightener, Lip Tightener)

Source: L02:AU_table

column1: Scaled dot-product attention: formula for score and context vector?
column2:
Score: s_t = (q · x_t) / √d
Weights: a_t = softmax(s_t)
Context: c = Σ_t a_t · x_t

Source: exam2024:Q3, L03:attention

column1: Why does the transformer need positional encoding?
column2:
Self-attention is permutation-invariant — without positional encoding, token order is lost.

Source: exam2024:Q1e, solution_ex1:3c

column1: How do RNNs capture long-range dependencies?
column2:
By maintaining a hidden state updated at each time step — acts as memory across the sequence.

Source: solution_ex1:3a

column1:
Exam 2024 — Attention: q=[1,0,1], x₁=[1,0,0], x₂=[0,1,0], x₃=[1,1,0], d=3.
Compute similarity scores s_t = (q·x_t)/√d.

Exam 2024 — Attention (continued): scores s₁=s₃=1/√3, s₂=0.
Compute attention weights via softmax.

Exam 2024 — Attention (continued): a₁≈0.398, a₂≈0.204, a₃≈0.398.
Compute context vector c = Σ a_t · x_t.
Neural network unit: formulas for z and output a?
column2:
z = w·x + b (weighted sum)
a = σ(z) (activation function applied to z)

Source: L03:neural_unit

column1: Exercise 1: w=[2.2,−3,1.5], x=[0,5,8], b=3, ReLU activation. Compute output.
column2:
z = 2.2·0 + (−3)·5 + 1.5·8 + 3 = 0
ReLU(0) = 0

Source: solution_ex1:3b

column1: Why do standard RNNs suffer from vanishing gradients?
column2:
Repeated multiplication of gradients through many time steps → gradients shrink exponentially, so long-range dependencies are hard to learn.

Source: exam2025:Q3a-ii, L03:rnn_problems

column1: LSTM gating mechanism — three gates and their role?
column2:
Forget gate: what to discard from cell state.
Input gate: what new info to store.
Output gate: what to output.
Cell state highway mitigates vanishing gradients.

Source: exam2025:Q3a-iii, L03:LSTM

column1: Encoder-decoder architecture — how does it work?
column2:
Encoder maps input sequence (x₁…x_T) to context vector h_T.
Decoder uses h_T to initialize autoregressive generation of output sequence (y₁…y_m).

Source: L03:encoder_decoder

column1: Teacher forcing vs. inference in encoder-decoder training?
column2:
Training: feed gold previous token y_{t−1} as decoder input.
Inference: feed model's own previous prediction ŷ_{t−1}.

Source: L03:teacher_forcing

column1: Self-attention vs. cross-attention in Transformers?
column2:
Self-attention: Q,K,V from same sequence (encoder or masked decoder).
Cross-attention: Q from decoder, K,V from encoder output.

Source: L03:attention, L03:transformers

column1: Why masked multi-head attention in the Transformer decoder?
column2:
Prevents positions from attending to future tokens — at test time future tokens are unknown (no cheating).

Source: L03:masked_attention

column1: Purpose of multi-head attention?
column2:
Multiple attention heads let the model attend to different types of relationships simultaneously (e.g., syntactic vs. semantic).

Source: L03:multi_head

column1: Transformer skip connections and layer normalization — why?
column2:
Skip connections: h_res = c + e — eases gradient flow.
Layer norm: normalizes across sequence dimension per example (not batch norm).

Source: L03:transformers

column1: Sinusoidal positional encoding: PE(t,2i) = {{c1::sin(t/10000^(2i/d))}}, PE(t,2i+1) = {{c2::cos(t/10000^(2i/d))}}
column2:
Added to token embedding: ê_t = e_t + PE(t). Gives unique position signal without recurrence.

Source: L03:positional_encoding, solution_ex1:3c

column1:
Exercise 1 attention: q=[3,1], k₁=[1,2], k₂=[2,0], d=2.
Compute scaled dot-product scores.
Exercise 1 attention (cont.): scores≈[3.54, 4.24]. Softmax weights?
column2:
a₁ = e^3.54/(e^3.54+e^4.24) ≈ 0.331
a₂ ≈ 0.669

Source: solution_ex1:3d

column1:
Exercise 1 attention (cont.): v₁=[0,3], v₂=[1,1], weights 0.331/0.669.
Context vector?

Exam 2025: X=[[1,2],[3,4]], PE(pos,0)=sin(pos), PE(pos,1)=cos(pos).
Compute X̂ (embeddings + PE) for positions 0 and 1.
Bidirectional RNN — how does it work?
column2:
Two RNNs: left-to-right and right-to-left on the sequence. Hidden states combined (concat/add/multiply) so each position sees both past and future context.

Source: L03:bidirectional_rnn

column1: RNN sequence classification — which hidden state is used?
column2:
Typically the last token's hidden state h_T (e.g., for sentiment classification).

Source: L03:sequence_classification

column1: What is backpropagation through time (BPTT)?
column2:
Unfold RNN over time steps and apply standard backprop across the unfolded graph to train shared weights U, V, W.

Source: L03:BPTT

column1: Exam 2025 Q3a: Why are RNNs suitable for sequential data?
column2:
They maintain a hidden state across time steps that summarizes past inputs — natural fit for text, speech, and video sequences.

Source: exam2025:Q3a-i

column1: RNN language modeling objective?
column2:
J = −Σ_t log P(y_t | x₁…x_t) — maximize probability of next token given history. Training uses teacher forcing.

Source: L03:language_modeling

column1: Cross-entropy loss for classification?
column2:
L = −Σ y_i · log(ŷ_i) — measures divergence between true distribution y and predicted ŷ. Used to train neural classifiers.

Source: L03:training

column1: Mel scale formula (Hz → Mel)?
column2:
Mel = 1127 · ln(1 + f/700)
Inverse: f = 700 · (e^(Mel/1127) − 1)

Source: exam2024:Q4, solution_ex1:4b

column1: What is Word Error Rate (WER)?
column2:
WER = (S + D + I) / N
S=substitutions, D=deletions, I=insertions, N=words in reference.
Lower is better.

Source: L04:wer, solution_ex3:1i

column1: Mel filter banks: triangular filters — what are start, peak, end?
column2:
For N filters, use N+2 equally spaced Mel points. Filter i peaks at point i+1, spans from point i to point i+2.

Source: exam2024:Q4, solution_ex1:4b

column1:
Exam 2024 Mel filters: 200–8000 Hz, 3 filters, 5 Mel points (i=0..4).
Compute Mel endpoints and first Hz point.
column2:
M_low = 1127·ln(1+200/700) ≈ 301 Mel
M_high = 1127·ln(1+8000/700) ≈ 2840 Mel
Δ = (2840−301)/4 ≈ 635
M₀ ≈ 301 → f₀ ≈ 200 Hz (check: matches lower bound)

Source: exam2024:Q4

column1: What is a phone in speech recognition?
column2:
A speech sound represented by a symbol (ARPAbet/IPA). A string of phones = pronunciation of a word.

Source: L04:phonetics

column1: Relationship between F0, pitch, and the Mel scale?
column2:
F0 = lowest frequency of periodic waveform. Pitch = perceptual correlate of F0 (not linear above ~1 kHz). Mel scale models perceived pitch spacing.

Source: L04:pitch

column1:
Exercise 1 speech: waveform period T=0.2 s, max amplitude A=2.
Compute frequency f and identify amplitude.
column2:
f = 1/T = 1/0.2 = 5 Hz
Amplitude A = 2 (max value on y-axis).

Source: solution_ex1:4a

column1: Speech feature extraction pipeline (after windowing)?
column2:
Window speech → DFT/FFT per frame → power spectrum → Mel filter bank → log → feature vector (e.g. 80-dim).

Source: L04:feature_extraction

column1: Typical windowing parameters for speech?
column2:
Window size: 20–40 ms. Stride: ~50% overlap. Hamming window reduces spectral leakage at frame edges.

Source: L04:windowing

column1: Hamming window formula and purpose?
column2:
w(n) = 0.54 − 0.46·cos(2πn/(N−1))
Tapers frame edges → minimizes discontinuities and spectral leakage.

Source: L04:hamming

column1: Mel filter bank construction — 5 steps?
column2:
1. Convert f_low, f_high to Mel
2. Space N+2 points linearly in Mel
3. Convert back to Hz
4. Round to FFT bins
5. Create overlapping triangular filters (peak at center point)

Source: L04:mel_construction, exam2024:Q4

column1:
Exercise 1 Mel: 100–2000 Hz, 4 filters (6 Mel points).
M_low and M_high?
column2:
M_low = 1127·ln(1+100/700) ≈ 150.36 Mel
M_high = 1127·ln(1+2000/700) ≈ 1521.45 Mel

Source: solution_ex1:4b

column1:
Exercise 1 Mel (cont.): ΔM=(1521.45−150.36)/5≈274.22.
List M₀…M₅ and Hz equivalents.
Exercise 1 Mel: define 4 triangular filters from 6 Hz points.
column2:
Filter 1: 100–320–600 Hz
Filter 2: 320–600–959 Hz
Filter 3: 600–959–1415 Hz
Filter 4: 959–1415–2000 Hz

Source: solution_ex1:4b

column1:
Exam 2024 Mel: 200–8000 Hz, 3 filters, 5 Mel points.
List all 5 Hz boundary points.
Exercise 1 WER case 1: REF has 6 words, 2 substitutions (a→at, towel→all). WER?
column2:
WER = 100 × (2+0+0) / 6 = 33.3%

Source: solution_ex1:4c

column1: Exercise 1 WER case 2: REF has 8 words, 2 substitutions + 1 insertion. WER?
column2:
WER = 100 × 3/8 = 37.5%

Source: solution_ex1:4c

column1: CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) — key ideas?
column2:
Outputs per frame incl. blank token. Collapse consecutive duplicates → many alignments map to same output. Training uses dynamic programming over alignments.

Source: L04:CTC

column1: RNN-Transducer — three components?
column2:
1. Encoder (acoustic model)
2. Predictor (language model, conditions on output so far)
3. Joiner (feedforward network combining encoder + predictor)

Source: L04:RNN-T

column1: Why does RNN-Transducer support streaming?
column2:
Online decoding: increment output index u on non-blank token, increment acoustic index t on blank — processes audio incrementally.

Source: L04:RNN-T, exam2024:Q1b

column1: Speech training augmentation — three types?
column2:
Time warping (distort time axis)
Frequency masking (mask mel bands)
Time masking (mask time frames)

Source: L04:augmentation

column1: Analog-to-digital conversion — two steps?
column2:
Sampling: discrete time points.
Quantization: map continuous amplitudes to finite discrete values (can be positive or negative).

Source: solution_ex1:1g, L04:adc

column1: Why subsample acoustic features in encoder-decoder ASR?
column2:
Input acoustic sequence is much longer than output token sequence — subsampling (low frame rate, CNN pooling, pyramidal RNN) compresses time dimension.

Source: L04:subsampling

column1: WER: define substitution, insertion, deletion.
column2:
Substitution: wrong word in place of REF word.
Insertion: extra word in SYS not in REF.
Deletion: REF word missing from SYS.

Source: L04:WER, solution_ex3:1i

column1:
Exam 2024 Mel: Hz points 200, 887, 2098, 4234, 8000 (3 filters).
Define filter 1 and filter 2 (start–peak–end).
column2:
Filter 1: 200 – 887 – 2098 Hz
Filter 2: 887 – 2098 – 4234 Hz
Filter 3: 2098 – 4234 – 8000 Hz

Source: exam2024:Q4

column1: Prosody in speech — what does it include?
column2:
Pitch (F0), duration, loudness, rhythm, intonation, prominence — NOT just spectral/formant features.

Source: L04:prosody, exam2025:Q1d

column1: Whisper ASR architecture type?
column2:
Encoder-decoder Transformer trained on large-scale weakly supervised speech data (multilingual, multitask).

Source: L04:Whisper

column1: Greedy decoding vs. top-k vs. top-p (nucleus) sampling?
column2:
Greedy: always pick highest prob token (deterministic, repetitive).
Top-k: sample from k highest-prob tokens.
Top-p: sample from smallest set with cumulative prob ≥ p.

Source: exam2024:Q5, solution_ex2:1a

column1: Three disadvantages of fine-tuning over prompt engineering?
column2:
1. Requires labeled data and significant compute
2. Risk of catastrophic forgetting
3. Less flexible to update — must retrain for new tasks

Source: exam2024:Q5b-i

column1: Name three prompt engineering strategies and how they work.
column2:
Zero-shot CoT: add 'Let's think step by step' to elicit reasoning.
Few-shot: provide input-output examples in prompt.
Self-ask: decompose into sub-questions before answering.

Source: exam2024:Q5b-ii, solution_ex2:2d

column1: Exam 2024 prompt failure: model mimics few-shot format instead of answering. Why and fix?
column2:
Problem: Model copies few-shot pattern (Question/Answer/Hint) instead of counting.
Reason: Trained on internet text with Q&A formats.
Fix: Clearer instructions, chain-of-thought, or fine-tune on task-specific data.

Source: exam2024:Q5b-iii

column1: Perplexity formula?
column2:
PP = 2^(−(1/N) · Σ log₂ P(w_i))
Lower perplexity = model is less 'surprised' by the text.

Source: exam2025:Q5c-ii, L06:perplexity

column1:
Exam 2025 — Top-p with p=0.85. Token probs: cat=0.35, dog=0.30, runs=0.15, fast=0.10, tree=0.06, blue=0.04.
List candidate tokens.
column2:
Cumulative: 0.35 → 0.65 → 0.80 → 0.90 ≥ 0.85
Candidates: cat, dog, runs, fast (stop when cum ≥ 0.85)

Source: exam2025:Q5b-ii, solution_ex2:2c

column1: Exam 2025 — Perplexity of 'The cat sits quietly.' Token probs: 0.20, 0.10, 0.15, 0.25.
TTS pipeline stages (in order)?
column2:
1. Text normalization
2. Phonetic analysis (G2P)
3. Prosodic analysis
4. Acoustic model (mel spectrogram)
5. Vocoder (waveform)

Source: L08:pipeline, solution_ex3

column1: What is a diphone? Why preferred over phones?
column2:
A diphone spans half of each adjacent phone — captures coarticulation transitions. Sounds more natural than isolated phones.

Source: exam2024:Q6a-i

column1: Unit selection: target cost vs. join cost?
column2:
Target cost: how well a unit matches desired acoustic features.
Join cost: discontinuity at unit boundaries.
Minimize total cost to select best sequence.

Source: exam2024:Q6a-ii

column1: Tacotron 2 vs. FastSpeech — key architectural difference?
column2:
Tacotron 2: autoregressive decoder + attention (implicit duration).
FastSpeech: non-autoregressive + duration predictor (parallel decoding).

Source: exam2024:Q6b-ii, solution_ex3:2a

column1: Griffin-Lim vs. WaveNet vs. HiFi-GAN?
column2:
Griffin-Lim: iterative phase recovery, no training, fast but robotic.
WaveNet: autoregressive neural, high quality, slow.
HiFi-GAN: GAN vocoder, parallel, real-time, near WaveNet quality.

Source: exam2024:Q6b-iii, solution_ex3:3a

column1: DiffWave: forward process, reverse process, conditioning?
column2:
Forward: add Gaussian noise over steps.
Reverse: iteratively denoise, predict noise at each step.
Conditioned on: mel spectrogram.

Source: exam2025:Q6b-ii, solution_ex3:3d

column1: Why learned speaker embeddings over one-hot in multi-speaker TTS?
column2:
Embeddings capture continuous similarity between speakers, enable interpolation, and generalize better than sparse one-hot vectors.

Source: exam2025:Q6b-iii, solution_ex3:4a

column1: Standard format for knowledge graphs?
column2:
RDF triples: (subject, predicate, object) — e.g. (Dr. Clark, affiliatedWith, CS Dept).

Source: exam2024:Q7a, L12:kg

column1: SPARQL: what is it used for?
column2:
W3C standard query language for RDF knowledge graphs. Pattern matching with SELECT/WHERE.

Source: exam2024:Q7b, solution_ex5:2b

column1: Massive incomplete KG: what approach instead of exact SPARQL?
column2:
Knowledge graph embeddings (TransE, TransR) — represent entities/relations as vectors for link prediction and similarity queries.

Source: exam2024:Q7b, L12:kge

column1: TransE scoring function?
column2:
For triple (h, r, t): score = ||h + r − t|| (L1 or L2 norm).
Lower score = more likely true. Training: minimize score for true triples.

Source: exam2025:Q7c, solution_ex5:2c

column1: Why can't TransE represent symmetric relations well?
column2:
TransE models r as translation: h + r ≈ t. For symmetric r: h + r ≈ t AND t + r ≈ h implies r ≈ 0 — can't capture meaningful symmetric relations.

Source: exam2025:Q7c-iii

h + r = [1.8, 0.8, −0.2] = t
h + r − t = [0, 0, 0]
||h+r−t||₂ = 0.0 (well below threshold 2.0 → likely true)

Source: exam2025:Q7c-i

column1: Define MDP components: state, action, reward, policy, return.
column2:
State s: environment observation
Action a: agent's choice
Reward r: scalar feedback
Policy π(a|s): action distribution
Return R: discounted sum of rewards

Source: exam2024:Q8, exam2025:Q8, L14:mdp

column1: Q(s,a) and V(s) relationship?
column2:
Q(s,a) = expected return from taking action a in state s.
V(s) = E_{a~π}[Q(s,a)] = Σ_a π(a|s)·Q(s,a).

Source: exam2025:Q8c, L14:value_functions

column1: Three advantages of PPO + Actor-Critic over vanilla policy gradient?
column2:
1. Critic baseline reduces variance
2. Clipped surrogate prevents destructively large updates
3. Sample efficiency via multiple epochs on same batch

Source: exam2025:Q8d, L14:ppo

column1: Why use RL instead of supervised learning for physics simulation tasks?
column2:
Simulation is typically non-differentiable — can't backprop through physics. RL learns from trial-and-error rewards.

Source: solution_ex5:3a, L14:applications

column1: Forward kinematics vs. inverse kinematics?
column2:
FK: given joint angles → compute end-effector position.
IK: given target position → compute joint angles.

Source: solution_ex4:2a, exam2024:Q9

column1: Jacobian IK update formula?
column2:
Δθ = α · J⁺ · Δe
where Δe = target − current end-effector, J⁺ = pseudoinverse of Jacobian, α = learning rate.

Source: solution_ex4:2b, exam2024:Q9

column1: What is the candy-wrapper artifact in LBS?
column2:
At large joint rotations, linear blend skinning causes unnatural twisting and volume loss around the joint axis.

Source: solution_ex4:2a5, exam2024:Q1s

column1: What is DeepPhase's core idea?
column2:
Learns phase-aware periodic motion representations via autoencoder — captures motion manifolds for data-driven animation synthesis and editing.

Source: exam2024:Q9b, exam2025:Q9b-i, solution_ex4:3a

column1: Normalizing flows — main idea and one animation application?
column2:
Learn invertible mappings from simple prior to complex data distribution; exact likelihood via Jacobian determinant.
Application: diverse motion generation with temporal consistency.

Source: exam2025:Q9b-ii, solution_ex4:1h

column1:
Exam 2024 IK: L1=5, L2=4, θ₁=θ₂=π/2, target [5,5], α=0.1.
Compute initial end-effector e₀.
column2:
e₀ = [5·cos(π/2)+4·cos(π), 5·sin(π/2)+4·sin(π)]
= [0+(−4), 5+0] = [−4, 5]

Source: exam2024:Q9a-i, solution_ex4:2b

column1: Exam 2024 IK (continued): e₀=[−4,5], target [5,5]. Compute Δe.
column2:
Δe = [5,5] − [−4,5] = [9, 0]

Source: exam2024:Q9a-ii, solution_ex4:2b

Δθ = 0.1 · J⁺ · Δe = 0.1 · [−1.8, 1.8] = [−0.18, 0.18]

Source: exam2024:Q9a-iv, solution_ex4:2b
